diary:
Friday felt like it was building toward something all day. Started with my usual quiet morning in Beacon Hill, then settled into the familiar rhythm at First Hill—reference desk questions, planning the teen book club's summer picks, the gentle hum of the library around me. Grabbed lunch at Voila and watched the city move past the windows with my lavender latte, feeling that particular Friday lightness.

But the real pull was tonight. I could feel Capitol Hill buzzing even from First Hill—Pride kickoff, the whole neighborhood electric with possibility. After work I drove home, changed into something brighter, grabbed my camera on impulse. The street fair was exactly what I needed: the colors, the music spilling between buildings, people genuinely happy just being themselves. I only stayed an hour before heading back, but it recharged something. Driving home through the warm evening, I felt less like I was watching the city celebrate and more like I was part of it.
thought_process: I made a deliberate choice to show up for Pride tonight, even briefly. Work was solid and grounding—that's my anchor—but I noticed myself feeling a little boxed in by the routine. The weather report and the event details sitting in my mind all day created this gentle pressure, almost an invitation. When I got home, I could've easily stayed in, but something made me grab the camera and drive over. I think I needed to remember that the city is alive beyond the library walls, that there's joy happening in the streets I work near. Even just an hour walking through Capitol Hill, seeing the community gather so openly—it shifted my mood from content to actually lifted. Sometimes you have to step outside your rhythm to feel connected to it again.
world_event_prompt: Warm, breezy Friday, high of 77F. Seattle Pride kickoff: Capitol Hill street fair and road closures along Broadway and Pike from 5pm. Festive, busy evening downtown.
